title: Streaming Service Cascade Testing with Geographic Mismatch
narrative: Fraudster validates stolen card credentials through sequential streaming platform subscriptions, exploiting free trial periods and low-value authorization holds. Pattern begins with $0.99-$1.99 authorization holds at 3-5 major streaming services (Netflix, Hulu, Disney+, HBO Max, Spotify) within 45-90 minute window during off-peak hours. Cardholder billing address (addr1) maps to Iowa or Nebraska while device geolocation (dist1) shows 800+ mile separation to urban center like Miami or Los Angeles. Email domains cycle through temporary providers (P_emaildomain: guerrillamail.com, 10minutemail.net). Successful validations typically escalate to higher-value entertainment MCCs within 2-4 hours. TransactionAmt progression shows distinct stairstepping: $0.99, $1.00, $1.99, $8.99, $14.99. All transactions occur ProductCD=S (subscription service) with identical card1 but rotating P_emaildomain values, indicating automated credential stuffing toolkit.
indicators: 4-6 streaming service MCCs (5815, 7841) within 90-minute window, all $0.99-$1.99 holds
dist1 exceeds 800 miles between billing addr1 and transaction origin
P_emaildomain rotates across 3+ disposable providers (guerrillamail, tempmail, 10minutemail)
All transactions occur txn_hour between 1-5 AM EST (is_night=1)
TransactionAmt shows stairstepping pattern: $0.99, $1.00, $1.99 within first 3 transactions
ProductCD=S for all transactions with identical card1 but zero R_emaildomain matches to P_emaildomain
summary: Multiple small streaming service charges ($0.99-$1.99) hitting the same card within 90 minutes during overnight hours, with the card's billing address hundreds of miles from where transactions originate and different throwaway email addresses used each time.
reasoning: Criminals test stolen card details through low-dollar streaming trials that rarely trigger alerts, using geographic distance and disposable emails to hide their location while confirming the card works before moving to larger fraudulent purchases.
indicator_explanations: Multiple streaming services hit with tiny test charges in rapid succession
Billing address and transaction location are separated by extreme geographic distance
Card suddenly becomes active after being completely dormant for a full day
Email rotates through multiple temporary disposable email providers across attempts
All activity clusters in the dead of night when cardholders rarely shop
Charge amounts escalate in deliberate small increments testing authorization limits
Service purchases use mismatched billing and recipient emails on same card
